narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 54.9% chance that you would not pass—crossing the threshold we use to make admission decisions. This prediction draws from patterns in data from thousands of past students, focusing on academic and background factors like grades, test scores, and family circumstances, without considering gender or race. Among the key elements shaping this outcome for you, your Year 3 school decile ranking stood out as the strongest positive influence, suggesting your performance there aligned well with those who typically pass the bar and helped pull the prediction toward success. However, your LSAT score had a notable push in the opposite direction, as it fell below the average of 37.27 seen among students who passed, making the model more concerned about bar exam struggles. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 4 contributed toward the failure prediction, especially since passers averaged around 6 in that measure, signaling early academic hurdles that weighed heavily. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, lower than the 3.24 average for successful bar takers, and upper-middle family income—which is common among passers—played supporting roles but weren't the top drivers. As a full-time student, that actually matches the norm for those who succeed, offering a small boost in the model's eyes. Compared to typical admitted students, your profile shows strengths in later-year performance and a solid family background, but the lower LSAT and early grades created a risk profile that tipped the scales. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many with similar starting points have improved dramatically. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to aim closer to 37 or higher, bolstering your transcript with stronger recent grades, and perhaps seeking targeted bar prep resources early. Reapplying after addressing these could shift your predicted odds significantly, opening doors at our or other programs. You're clearly capable, especially with your Year 3 momentum, so building on that foundation will make a real difference.
